players.name: Angus Gunn
players.bio: Son of former Scotland keeper Bryan Gunn, Angus carries the family trade with quiet authority at Nottingham Forest. A commanding presence with sharp reflexes, he's earned his place as Scotland's first-choice stopper for this historic tournament.
players.facts: Goalkeeping is literally in his DNA — his dad Bryan kept goal for Scotland 6 times in the late 80s.

players.name: Aaron Hickey
players.bio: A marauding full-back who made his name in Serie A before landing at Brentford, Hickey combines tenacity with technical quality. Still only 24, he's one of Scotland's most exciting defenders and a constant threat going forward.
players.facts: Was playing for Bologna in Italy at just 19 — not bad for a lad from Edinburgh.

players.name: Andy Robertson
players.bio: Scotland's captain and heartbeat, Robertson has been one of world football's finest left-backs for a decade at Liverpool. Approaching 100 caps, his engine, delivery, and leadership make him the Tartan Army's talisman at this World Cup.
players.facts: Was rejected by Celtic as a teenager and worked in a ticket office. Now he's played Champions League finals.

players.name: Scott McTominay
players.bio: The midfield colossus who swapped Manchester for Naples — and thrived. McTominay's goals, grit, and box-to-box brilliance make him Scotland's most influential player. With 15 international goals, he's the engine room and the danger man rolled into one.
players.facts: His father Frank is English, making Scott eligible for England — thankfully for Scotland, he chose the kilt.

players.name: Grant Hanley
players.bio: A veteran centre-back with 68 caps, Hanley has been Scotland's defensive anchor for years. Now at Hibernian, the Dumfries-born stopper brings experience and aerial dominance, marshalling the backline with calm authority at his first World Cup.
players.facts: Skippered Norwich City in the Premier League — not once, but across two separate top-flight spells.

players.name: Kieran Tierney
players.bio: One of Scotland's most beloved players, Tierney attacks and defends with equal ferocity. His return to Celtic reignited his career after injury frustrations in England. A warrior in a left-back's shirt, and pure box office for the Tartan Army.
players.facts: KT once played an entire match with a broken nose and two cracked ribs. He didn't mention it until after.

players.name: John McGinn
players.bio: McGinn is Scotland's beating heart — a box-to-box dynamo with a ferocious shot and an infectious will to win. With 20 international goals and 86 caps, the Aston Villa stalwart arrives at his first World Cup as one of the squad's key leaders.
players.facts: Began his career at St Mirren in the Scottish Championship. A true lower-league-to-World-Cup story.

players.name: Tyler Fletcher
players.bio: A teenage prodigy at Manchester United, Fletcher is Scotland's most exciting new face at this World Cup. Just 19 with only 2 caps, his technical ability and composure beyond his years have fast-tracked him onto the grandest stage of all.
players.facts: At 19, he's one of the youngest players at the entire 2026 World Cup — born the year Scotland last qualified.

players.name: Lyndon Dykes
players.bio: Born in Australia, raised to be Scottish, Dykes is pure physical force up front. The Charlton striker battles defenders into submission and holds the ball like a man who enjoys the combat. Ten goals in 51 caps tells its own story.
players.facts: Born in Queensland, Australia — he qualifies for Scotland through his Scottish father. G'day, Tartan Army.

players.name: Ché Adams
players.bio: Born in Leicester, Ché chose Scotland and never looked back, delivering 13 goals in 47 caps. The Torino forward's pace, link-up play and eye for goal make him a handful for any defence. A smart, versatile forward built for the big stage.
players.facts: Ché was born in Leicester but eligible for Scotland through his Scottish mother. Buonasera from Torino.

players.name: Ryan Christie
players.bio: An Inverness lad who made it all the way to the Premier League with Bournemouth, Christie is a creative spark in midfield with a cultured left foot. Ten goals in 68 caps underline his quality, and he thrives when games open up.
players.facts: Christie once scored a stunning long-range free kick against Israel in a World Cup qualifier. The Tartan Army still talk about it.

players.name: Liam Kelly
players.bio: Born in Basingstoke but proudly Scottish, Kelly is a combative central midfielder who brings steel and structure to Rangers' midfield. With only 3 caps, this World Cup is a huge opportunity to announce himself on the international stage.
players.facts: Born in England, plays in Scotland, representing Scotland at a World Cup in North America. Truly a citizen of everywhere.

players.name: Jack Hendry
players.bio: The Glasgow-born centre-back has taken an unusual path via Belgium and Saudi Arabia to reach his first World Cup. Hendry is a commanding defender who reads the game well and offers a threat at set-pieces with 3 international goals.
players.facts: Hendry played for Club Brugge in Belgium before heading to the Saudi Pro League — quite the passport collection.

players.name: Ross Stewart
players.bio: A powerful, old-fashioned centre-forward from Glasgow, Stewart has battled injury to reach this World Cup with Southampton. His aerial ability and hold-up play give Scotland a different dimension, and he's hungry to make his mark with just 3 caps.
players.facts: Ross Stewart once scored 24 goals in a single Championship season for Sunderland. The fans still sing his name.

players.name: John Souttar
players.bio: Aberdeen-born Souttar is a composed, ball-playing centre-back who has been a reliable presence for Rangers and Scotland alike. Two international goals from defence highlight his threat at set-pieces. A steady hand in a squad full of flair.
players.facts: His brother Harry Souttar plays for Australia — making them one of football's great international sibling rivalries.

players.name: Dominic Hyam
players.bio: Dundee-born Hyam has carved out a remarkable story, rising through lower league football to earn his Scotland caps and reach a World Cup with Wrexham. A solid, no-nonsense centre-back who represents the spirit of football's underdog tale.
players.facts: He plays for Wrexham — the club made globally famous by Ryan Reynolds. Yes, the Deadpool guy owns his club.

players.name: Ben Gannon-Doak
players.bio: Dalry's finest export, Ben Doak is a dazzling right winger whose direct running and fearless attitude make him Scotland's most electric wide threat. Just 20 years old at this World Cup, the Bournemouth flyer is one of the tournament's must-watch talents.
players.facts: Doak was still a teenager when he started turning heads at Celtic Park and then Liverpool's academy. The sky's the limit.

players.name: George Hirst
players.bio: Sheffield-born with Scottish eligibility, Hirst carries serious footballing pedigree as the son of Leeds legend David Hirst. A physical, energetic forward at Ipswich Town, he's determined to use his 10 caps to build a World Cup legacy of his own.
players.facts: His father David Hirst was a Sheffield Wednesday legend famous for one of the hardest shots ever recorded in English football.

players.name: Lewis Ferguson
players.bio: A dynamic midfielder who has impressed massively in Serie A with Bologna, Ferguson brings energy, vision and a nose for goal to Scotland's engine room. The nephew of former Rangers midfielder Barry Ferguson, football runs deep in his family.
players.facts: Lewis is the nephew of Barry Ferguson, one of Rangers' greatest ever captains. Midfield talent is hereditary in this family.

players.name: Lawrence Shankland
players.bio: Lawrence Shankland is a natural-born goalscorer who has terrorised Scottish Premiership defences for years at Hearts. Seven goals in 20 caps show he can translate that form internationally, and at 30 this World Cup could be the pinnacle of his career.
players.facts: Shankland has been the Scottish Premiership's top scorer multiple times. Hearts fans call him their 'Scottish Messi' — they're biased but not entirely wrong.

players.name: Craig Gordon
players.bio: At 43, Craig Gordon is football's answer to fine wine — still sharp, still agile, and still Scotland's most experienced keeper. With 84 caps and a career that survived a near-catastrophic leg break, his presence at this World Cup is the stuff of legend.
players.facts: Gordon suffered a devastating leg fracture in 2018 that nearly ended his career. He came back to play more than 100 games. Remarkable.

players.name: Nathan Patterson
players.bio: A pacy, industrious right-back who came through the Rangers academy, Patterson has developed into a reliable international defender for Everton. Still only 24 with 26 caps, this World Cup is the perfect stage for him to prove his top-level credentials.
players.facts: Nathan Patterson once scored a World Cup qualifier goal — which for a right-back is practically a national celebration.

players.name: Kenny McLean
players.bio: A dependable and experienced central midfielder, McLean has given Scotland sterling service across 58 caps and has been a regular figure in the squad for years. The Norwich man's positional intelligence and passing range make him a trusted midfield option.
players.facts: Kenny McLean played for Aberdeen, St Mirren and Norwich across his career — a true Scottish football journeyman done good.

players.name: Anthony Ralston
players.bio: A Celtic academy product who became a first-team regular through sheer determination, Ralston is a dynamic right-back with a love of getting forward. His 27 caps represent a journey from fringe player to established international, and he's not done yet.
players.facts: Ralston was almost released by Celtic before breaking into the first team. He's now a Champions League regular. Never give up.

players.name: Findlay Curtis
players.bio: Kilmarnock's teenage sensation, Curtis is one of the most exciting young forwards in Scottish football. Already with a Scotland goal to his name from just 3 caps, the 20-year-old is a wildcard pick with genuine World Cup ambitions and electric pace.
players.facts: Curtis scored on his first Scotland appearance — an exclusive club that some players never get to join in 50+ caps.

players.name: Scott McKenna
players.bio: A no-nonsense centre-back who has plied his trade across England, Scotland and Croatia with Dinamo Zagreb, McKenna brings versatility and experience to Scotland's defensive options. Half a century of caps proves he's been a consistent servant to the cause.
players.facts: McKenna swapped Nottingham Forest for Croatian giants Dinamo Zagreb — not the most common career move in football history.
